skill.name: game-audio
skill.description: Game audio design principles covering sound effects, music integration, and adaptive audio systems for immersive gameplay.
skill.summary: Learn professional game audio design including SFX layering, music state systems, 3D spatialization, and platform optimization.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is documentation-only content providing game audio design principles. Static analysis flagged 8 patterns that are all false positives: Markdown code fences (```) were misidentified as shell backtick execution, and text patterns were incorrectly flagged as cryptographic algorithms. The skill contains no executable code, only read-only tool usage (Read, Glob, Grep) for accessing documentation content.

content.user_title: Master game audio design for immersive gameplay
content.value_statement: Game developers struggle with creating engaging audio experiences that enhance gameplay. This skill provides structured principles for sound design, music integration, and adaptive audio systems used in professional game production.
actual_capabilities: Explain audio category systems and priority hierarchies for game sound mixing
Guide sound design decisions including recording, synthesis, and layering techniques
Design music state systems with appropriate transition techniques for dynamic gameplay
Implement adaptive audio based on game parameters like threat level and environment
Apply 3D spatialization principles for positional audio in game scenes
Recommend platform-specific audio formats and memory budget strategies
limitations: Provides design principles only; does not generate actual audio files
Cannot integrate with specific game engines like Unity or Unreal directly
Does not replace professional audio editing software or DAW tools
Focuses on conceptual guidance rather than implementation code

use_cases.title: Indie game developer designing audio system
use_cases.description: Plan and structure your game's audio architecture from scratch using professional principles for categories, priorities, and mixing.

use_cases.title: Sound designer creating game assets
use_cases.description: Learn layering techniques and platform considerations to create effective sound effects that work within technical constraints.

use_cases.title: Game designer prototyping mechanics
use_cases.description: Understand how adaptive audio responds to game states to design more immersive gameplay experiences.

prompt_templates.title: Basic audio category setup
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm creating a 2D platformer game. Help me set up audio categories and define priority levels for different sound types like player jumps, enemy sounds, background music, and collectible pickups.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setting up first game audio

prompt_templates.title: Sound effect layering guide
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to create a gunshot sound effect for my FPS game. Guide me through the layering process with attack, body, tail, and sweetener components.

prompt_templates.title: Adaptive music system design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an adaptive music system for my action RPG that transitions between exploration, combat, and boss phases. Include vertical layering and horizontal segment approaches.

prompt_templates.title: Platform optimization strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: My mobile game audio exceeds the 50 MB budget. Help me optimize audio formats, compression settings, and asset strategies while maintaining acceptable quality.

output_examples.input: What audio categories should I use for a racing game?
output: Music: Engine soundtrack that intensifies with speed
SFX: Engine sounds, tire screeches, collisions (3D positioned)
Ambient: Crowd noise, wind, environment sounds
UI: Menu selections, lap notifications, position updates

output_examples.input: How do I handle audio ducking when dialogue plays?
output: Reduce music volume by 6-9 dB when voice starts
Apply smooth fade over 100-200ms to avoid abrupt changes
Duck ambient sounds by 3-6 dB for clarity
Return to original levels 500ms after dialogue ends

best_practices: Use 3-5 variations per sound effect to prevent repetitive audio fatigue
Implement proper mix hierarchy with voice as the reference level at 0 dB
Design audio transitions to occur at musically appropriate points like beat boundaries
anti_patterns: Playing the exact same sound repeatedly without variation
Setting all audio to maximum volume without proper mix balance
Ignoring the role of silence in creating audio contrast and impact

faq.question: What audio file formats should I use for my game?
faq.answer: Use OGG Vorbis for PC games, platform-specific formats for consoles, MP3 or AAC for mobile, and WebM/Opus with MP3 fallback for web games. Consider quality versus file size tradeoffs.

faq.question: How much memory should I allocate for game audio?
faq.answer: Mobile casual games should target 10-50 MB, PC indie games 100-500 MB, and AAA titles can exceed 1 GB. Plan your strategy based on compression and variant needs.

faq.question: What is the difference between vertical and horizontal adaptive audio?
faq.answer: Vertical layering adds or removes instrument tracks based on intensity. Horizontal uses different music sections or segments. Combined systems use both approaches for maximum flexibility.

faq.question: Should UI sounds be 3D positioned?
faq.answer: No, UI sounds should remain non-positional as they provide interface feedback, not gameplay information. Keep them consistent regardless of camera position.

faq.question: How do I prevent audio repetition in looped music?
faq.answer: Create multiple loopable sections, use stem mixing for variety, implement transition stingers between states, and consider generative or procedural elements for extended play sessions.

faq.question: What is audio ducking and when should I use it?
faq.answer: Ducking temporarily reduces volume of certain audio categories when others play. Use it when dialogue needs clarity (duck music), during explosions (duck everything briefly), or when menus open (duck gameplay audio).
